justification: All pass criteria met: Breaking change clearly identified ('This is not a minor enhancement — it redefines what a Shipment *is*'). New minimum-cardinality invariant stated (BR-1). Data migration impact for 2M records noted (BR-7, AC-4). External API breakage identified (BR-8, AC-5). 'Delivery preferences' ambiguity surfaced as first escalation. Architectural concerns flagged via escalations (though MJ not mentioned by name, the escalations clearly call for architectural decisions). Confidence level 45, well below the 75 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough domain analysis — goes well beyond minimum requirements with 9 business rules, 8 acceptance criteria, and 7 edge cases
Correctly identifies the cardinality change as a breaking structural change to the core aggregate, not a simple additive change
BR-1 explicitly states the minimum-one-recipient invariant with strong justification
BR-7 addresses data migration for 2M records with byte-for-byte preservation requirement
BR-8 and AC-5 address external API backward compatibility with a clear v1/v2 versioning strategy
Delivery preferences ambiguity surfaced as a formal escalation with concrete options and recommendation
Confidence at 45 is appropriately low given the number of undefined business decisions
Introduces the Primary Recipient concept proactively for backward compatibility — shows deep domain thinking
Business impact section is comprehensive, covering financial, operational, user, and risk dimensions with 7 stakeholder groups
Edge cases cover non-obvious scenarios like billing model, delivery zone constraints, and mid-transit mutability
Escalations are well-structured with options and recommendations, not just problem statements
Does not explicitly reference MJ by name for architectural migration planning — escalations imply architectural work but don't delegate to a specific agent
The existing invariant change ('A Shipment has exactly one Recipient' becoming false) is implicit in the analysis but not explicitly called out as an invariant transition from true to false

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: Breaking change clearly identified ('fundamental structural change to the aggregate root'), minimum-cardinality invariant stated (BR-1), data migration impact for 2M records noted (BR-4, business_impact.operational), external API breakage identified (BR-7, edge_cases), 'delivery preferences' ambiguity surfaced (escalation #4), architectural concerns flagged via escalations, and confidence.level=52 well within <=75 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough domain analysis — goes well beyond minimum requirements with ubiquitous language definitions, 8 business rules with invariant justifications, and 9 acceptance criteria
Correctly identifies the cardinality change as breaking and articulates cascading impacts across billing, POD, status tracking, notifications, and routing
BR-1 explicitly states the minimum-cardinality invariant with testable assertion for both creation and removal scenarios
Data migration impact addressed comprehensively — BR-4 covers data integrity, edge case covers idempotent/resumable migration, business_impact notes staged rollout with rollback
External API breakage thoroughly covered with BR-7, AC-6/AC-7, and partner webhook edge case — includes backward compatibility strategy options
Delivery preferences ambiguity surfaced as explicit escalation with concrete options and recommendation
Surfaces additional valuable ambiguities beyond requirements: billing model, status reconciliation, recipient mutability during lifecycle
Confidence level of 52 appropriately reflects the many unresolved business decisions, with well-articulated high/low confidence areas
Edge cases are practical and operationally grounded — duplicate recipients, mid-transit changes, partial refusal, migration failure recovery
Stakeholder impact analysis is comprehensive, covering 7 distinct groups with specific impacts for each

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: Breaking change clearly identified ('fundamental change to the aggregate root structure'); minimum-cardinality invariant stated (BR-1: 'A Shipment MUST have at least one Recipient'); data migration impact for 2M records noted (AC-8 and business_impact.risk); external API breakage identified (BR-8, AC-6, edge cases); 'delivery preferences' ambiguity surfaced as first escalation; architectural concerns flagged via multiple escalations suitable for MJ; confidence.level=52 well under 75 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough analysis — goes well beyond minimum requirements with 9 business rules, 9 acceptance criteria, 8 edge cases, and 5 escalations
Correctly identifies the change as breaking to the aggregate root structure, not a simple additive change
BR-1 explicitly states the new minimum-cardinality invariant with clear justification
Surfaces the delivery preferences ambiguity as a formal escalation with concrete options and a recommendation
External API breakage handled with anti-corruption layer concept (BR-8) and backward compatibility strategy
Data migration for 2M records addressed in both acceptance criteria (AC-8) and risk assessment
Confidence at 52 is appropriately low given the number of unresolved ambiguities
Identifies additional high-value ambiguities beyond the rubric requirements: billing model, recipient mutability mid-transit, max recipient count, privacy model
Correctly distinguishes invariants (physical/structural constraints) from policies (business decisions that can change)
Strong ubiquitous language section introduces necessary new terms like 'Primary Recipient', 'Recipient Delivery Status', and 'Multi-drop Shipment'
Does not explicitly call out MJ by name for architectural migration planning, though the escalations clearly scope architectural work that would be MJ's domain
Edge cases are practical and operationally grounded — billing, partial cancellation, mid-transit changes, bulk scenarios, analytics impact
